event: The Fall of the Rebel Angels
symbols: Michael with upraised sword — the champion of God's army, wielding divine authority
falling naked figures — the rebel angels stripped of glory and cast out of heaven
armor and wings — the martial splendor of the heavenly host
downward movement of the fallen — the descent from celestial glory to damnation
upper realm of light versus lower realm of darkness — the cosmic division between faithfulness and rebellion
description: A tall arched composition dominated by the figure of Archangel Michael at center, standing in armor with large dark wings spread wide, sword raised above his head. Around him, loyal angels assist in the battle. Below, numerous naked human-like figures tumble downward in contorted poses — the rebel angels being cast out of heaven. The upper portion is filled with clouds and light where the victorious angels reside; the lower portion descends into darkness where the defeated rebels fall. The composition has powerful vertical energy, moving from heavenly triumph above to infernal defeat below.
theological_description: The Fall of the Rebel Angels depicts one of the most dramatic events in biblical cosmology. Revelation describes the cosmic battle: 'Then war broke out in heaven. Michael and his angels fought against the dragon, and the dragon and his angels fought back. But he was not strong enough, and they lost their place in heaven. The great dragon was hurled down — that ancient serpent called the devil, or Satan, who leads the whole world astray. He was hurled to the earth, and his angels with him' (Revelation 12:7-9). This event is also reflected in Isaiah's oracle against the king of Babylon, traditionally applied to Satan's fall: 'How you have fallen from heaven, morning star, son of the dawn! You have been cast down to the earth' (Isaiah 14:12). Jesus himself testified to this primordial event: 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven' (Luke 10:18). The theological significance is immense — evil originates not in God's creation but in the free will of created beings who chose pride over obedience. Michael's name itself is a battle cry meaning 'Who is like God?' — the rhetorical question that exposes the absurdity of any creature's attempt to rival the Creator. The defeat of the rebels assures the Church that, however fierce the spiritual battle, the outcome has already been decided: 'They triumphed over him by the blood of the Lamb' (Revelation 12:11).

scripture_refs.reason: Michael and his angels warring against the dragon and casting the rebel angels out of heaven
The fall of the morning star from heaven — traditionally applied to Satan's primordial rebellion
Jesus seeing Satan fall like lightning from heaven — his testimony to this cosmic event
The triumph over the dragon by the blood of the Lamb — the assured victory this battle foreshadows
God did not spare angels when they sinned but sent them to hell — the fate of the falling figures

_meta.title: The Fall of the Rebel Angels
